question: Create an `Employee` class with `name` and `id`. Design a `Team` class that manages an array of `Employee` pointers to represent team members. Demonstrate adding employees to the team without the team owning their memory.

test_cases.expected:
Added employee Alice to team.
Added employee Bob to team.
Added employee Charlie to team.
Employee Alice is already in the team (pointer match).

--- Team Members ---
Employee ID: 101, Name: Alice
Employee ID: 102, Name: Bob
Employee ID: 103, Name: Charlie
--------------------

errors: The `Team::addEmployee` method performs a linear search (O(N) complexity) to check if an employee already exists. If duplicate pointers are acceptable, this check is unnecessary overhead. If uniqueness is required, using `std::set<Employee*>` or `std::unordered_set<Employee*>` would offer better average time complexity (O(log N) or O(1) respectively) for large teams.

Instead of using the more concise and idiomatic `teamMembers.push_back(emp);`, the `addEmployee` method manually resizes the vector and then assigns the element. This is less readable and potentially less efficient than `push_back` which is optimized for appending.

The `Team::displayTeam` method uses a traditional index-based `for` loop. A range-based `for` loop (`for (Employee* emp : teamMembers)`) would be more modern, concise, and readable for iterating over the entire collection.
